sentences.text: Because Athens was under the Italian occupation.
Instead of Salonika was under the German occupation, and the south of Greece under the Bulgarian.
It was divided in, into three parts.
It's-- their, their character is very similar to the Greek character from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], from the same part of, OF the world.
We speak moving our hands.
They do also.
They have many, many similar characteristics with the Greeks.
Yes.
They didn't care about the Jews.
They didn't have anything in mind of humiliating the Jews, didn't have any plans to put them in a concentration camp until the final solution.
It was not their goal.
It was not their purpose.
Yes.
Yes.
From Spain-- when Jews came from-- after the Spanish Inquisition, they brought the Spanish language, which deteriorated into Ladino.
If I go to Spain and I speak Ladino, everybody will understand me.
And they understand the language also.
It's very, very, very similar to the Spanish language.
And all of the Jews-- now there are just a few of us who speak Ladino.
The new generation, unfortunately, didn't learn Ladino.
It, it, it is an language that will distinguish in some years in our community.
In Athens, the Jews didn't speak Ladino.
It was always in Thessaloniki.
My husband, who comes from Drama, he didn't speak Ladino.
He learned the language living with my parents.
It was only the, the Jews of Salonika that spoken Ladino.
Yes.
I didn't go to school.
I remember very well, I didn't go to school.
Then after my father, as I told you, he was on the, the streets because he didn't want to stay in the-- an apartment.
He was not the kind of a person that would be locked up.
He was on the street, and the Germans caught him.
And he managed to call his director and tell him for my mother and me not to go to the community, to go into hiding.
And my mother's answer was no.
I will go wherever my husband is, and with my child.
This man, he tried to convince my mother to keep, to keep me in his home, saying that there was a niece from an island-- because they were original from an island.
But my mother wouldn't let me.
And I remember had a doll, and I ask the lady whom I called aunt to keep the doll for me.
And she kept it for me.
After the war, she gave me the doll.
And I went with my mother to of the Jewish community.
And from there, we were taken to Haidari.
Haidari was a prison where they took all the Jews.
We stayed there for eight days.
And then we were put on the train to Bergen-Belsen.
And our trip lasted 13 days, I remember very well.
It was 13 days.
Yes.
They, they put them in the community center, there at the community-- the Jewish community in Athens.
No, they were not living together.
They were rounded up.
Because it was the end of the war.
The Italians had-- were not the allies of the Germans anymore.
And so the Germans were the, the masters of Athens also.
So they rounded up all the Jews.
We went to meet him, went to meet him.
Yes, all the Jews, and we were the Jews that had Spanish, Italian, Yugoslavian, foreign identity, foreign nationality, not Greek Jews.
All of of us had foreign identities.
No.
No.
I didn't.
I'm sure my parents didn't know either.
No
They put us in the cattle cars that you all have seen.
And we were sitting on the floor.
We tried-- the families tried to keep together in little spaces.
There was a big net in the car where they had put some bread.
And there was a big can used for the latrine.
And the Germans will-- would open it not once a-- even less than once a day so that they could empty the latrine.
And I remember then my father used to take me in his arms.
And I would look out of the little window-- there is a very small window in these cattle cars with barbed wire-- to see the sky.
And everyone was praying in the car.
We, as children, I don't think I was frightened.
I didn't know anything what would happen of me.
I didn't know, until we arrived in Bergen-Belsen.
Yes, it was 14 day-- 13 days.
No, no. no, no, I don't remember anything like that.
No, I don't remember.
No, of course not-- who would have told us?
Nobody told us.
And at Bergen-Belsen, I was separated.
There was a camp with Jews from all the countries of the world, world that had been there for a long time.
There were the Jews that had their number tattooed on their hands and labored there and died.
But we were separated with Jews of other countries.
The last convey, we were the last, the last convey.
And we didn't have any numbers tattooed on our hands, not even wear the yellow star or the striped uniform.
We wore, wore our own clothes.
I remember when they took us from Athens, it was the 25th of March, 1944.

And since we stayed eight days in Haidari and 30 days-- 13 days in trip, it must be April, not-- we arrived in March 25, March 25, because we stayed there for three-- 13 months until we were liberated.
Our stay in Bergen-Belsen 13 months.
No, no, no, no.
We were in the same camp with my father, only different barracks.
Women and children were in different-- in, in certain barracks and men taken.
But we could see each other every day.
No.
No, we didn't work, either-- our camp, no.
My father did a lot of work, because he wanted to provide some more food for his wife and child.
But it was he wanted to work.
It-- he was not obliged to do so.
The last transport and Jews with foreign passports.
Yes, we could see many.
My father and mother met many friends from the other side of the fence, as we say, of the barbed wire, people that we knew, that knew from Salonika.
And many of them survived.
And they came back.
Of course when you live, you live all the day and night in a barrack with a lot of women and children, you come to know many people.
And I played with little girls.
Boys, I don't remember, with little girls.
And there was a lot of singing in the camp at night, especially at night, always sad songs about the home we lost, about our country.
And I remember there was only one man living in our barrack.
He was a, a philosopher.
His name was Joseph Nehama.
He has written a lot of books.
And he was the only man that had a bed in our barrack.
And I remember, he used to-- as a pastime, he used to write down recipes.
And the women would pass by.
And he would say, as to my mother, Alien, can you tell me a recipe?
They would write down a recipe even though we were so hungry, we couldn't even listen to that.
But I remember this scene that Mr.
Nehama kept writing down recipes.
We were friendly.
There were quarrels also, because every one wanted the best for his own people.
My father kept quarreling with all the people, all everybody.
And there was running after him all the time, and yelling to my mother come, come, father is squirrelling again.
And it was not because he had a bad character.
It was because he wanted to have some spare-- a piece of bread for his wife and child.
He was always trying to provide us with some more food.
He was a wonderful father.
I spoke Greek.
But I'm sure my parents spoke Ladino or French.
It, it was something very peculiar.
The Greek language overcame all of the language of the camp.
It means that whatever-- the Greeks gave name to the latrines.
We called it [GREEK SPEECH], because they were round holes with covers.
A cover in Greek means [GREEK SPEECH].
We called [GREEK SPEECH].
And all the inmates of all the, of all the countries called it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
I mean, the words that Greek-- the song, the Greek songs, they sang also.
The Greek language, even though we're not so many, was the language they had to communicate.
What I remember was a family from Holland.
And there was a teacher that gathered us, all the children.
And she even prepared with pieces of paper candles for Hanukkah.
She taught us [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was the song.
I even remember it today-- for Hanukkah.
She taught us about the, the religion and our holidays.
And even, she taught us how to sing and dance in Holland.
Other language, I don't remember.
But the, the Dutch language, I do, some song and the Dutch people.
There were people, very, very rich, of great families who were rounded, rounded up at the end, end of the war, from Holland.
It was not a singing happy singing.
It was always memories.
It was for the memories of the good old days.
And there was a lot of crying, a lot of sickness, and a lot, a lot of hunger, and cold.
I was sick.
I had smallpox in the camp with very high fever.
And they took me to the dispensary there.
And I remember that my mother had to sit on a bench-- it was not a bench, it was a little wooden bench, a little one.
And she slept there and stayed for weeks because I was very sick.
But I survived.
I think they called this good for people.
I survived.
Yes, she did stay with me.
Every morning, we had to go to the appell.
And appell meant we had to be present in the square in the camp so that they could count us.
I remember that that was an ordeal, because we had to stand up for a long, long hours in the cold.
And I don't remember men SS.
I remember the women very well, with the big dogs.
The women, I can see them till now.
The women was-- perhaps because I was a little girl and the women attracted me more, most, but I don't remember SS officers or soldiers.
It was always the women with blonde head, with these ugly faces, so, so cruel faces, and always with big shepherd dogs, the German Shepherd dogs.
Germans never talked, the Germans bark.
They don't talk.
It was always schwein, Juden, raus, the three words that I knew and heard at the camp.
It was pigs, Jews, and raus.
And schnell means quickly.
Schnell is quickly and raus, outside.
They don't talk.
They shout.
There was a Jewish community-- committee-- that, let us say, conducted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- gave the food, the portions of the food.
And not the well-being, but if you were sick, you would go to them.
If somebody quarrel with another, they were the ones that would take care of the matter.
They-- we had a, a small council.
I don't know.
There must be-- the inmates.
I don't know.
My father was not on the committee, quarrelling with them always.
I think they had to help their families first.
It's natural-- for me, it's natural.
But nothing bad about them was heard after the war.
Because after the war, you hear a lot of things about people you knew.
You hadn't seen with your own eyes what they did, but you hear about it.
No, nothing was heard about them.
They were in a-- no, no, no, no, no, no.
We tried to help them.
I remembered my mother tried to give them whatever spare clothes she had to them, especially to two friends that she knew very well.
They were her very dear friends and even relatives.
And she-- my father tried to smuggle some food to them from the barbed wire.
No, they were not against us, no.
Some.
Some.
Very little.
But we're not on-- in rags as the other Jews were.
No.
We went back to the barracks.
The children played with nothing, let us say.
We played.
And my mother used to make the beds.
And she would try to, to knit something, to undo something and re-knit it again.
And they will say stories of their lives.
There was nothing else to do, and always trying to find some more food for the children.
There was nothing else to do.
Of course, we were aware because we could hear the air raids.
And we would go out of the barracks.
And I remember very well that my parents prayed to God that the, the airplanes will drop bombs in the camp.
They wanted the Allied force to bomb the camp.
No.
I don't remember anything, them speaking about after the war.
No.
No.
It was-- the minute you could leave, the moment, the day, the hour that you could survive.
You couldn't think about the, the future.
I, as a child, I didn't care about it.
I'm sure that my parents counted the months and the days. and always having in mind what, what happened to their brothers, and sisters, and mothers, and all the relatives.
I remember that they tried to ask the other people from the upper side of the camp if they had seen-- have you seen my sister?
Have you seen my mother, people asking them.
We didn't have any track of them.
We didn't see them anymore.
We tried to find them through the Red Cross after the war.
Unfortunately, no one came back from our family.
Yes.
She tried to, to keep up the-- to teach us-- not a religion-- to even in the worse conditions, to know that we are Jewish and to know what Hanukkah meant.
I don't remember any other holiday.
What is kept in my mind very vividly is Hanukkah.
They took us and put us on a train.
And asking around, we found out they were taking us, taking us to Brandenburg.
I don't even know where it is, where it was then.
And we traveled for a lot.
There many days, we're on a train.
I could only see forests, a lot of trees, and the air attacks that succeeded one another.
So the Germans had to put white sheets of the trains, on top of the trains, so that the airplanes would see, would see that there were civilians.
It was not an army train, it was civilians.
And suddenly, the train stopped.
It was bombarded, but only the beginning of the convey, where the, the Germans, the German soldiers were and their-- their guns were there.
But they didn't bombard the wagons with the white sheets on, because they understood.
The, the planes flew very, very low.
So they could, they could see that there was civilians and didn't bombard them.
And the, the Dutch people, I don't know how, they had a lot of jewelry with them, a lot of diamonds.
So they bargained with the Germans.
They gave them the jewelry.
And they fled and left us there, left us in the middle of the forest, just left us there.
So we were free.
We went out to seek for food.
It was in April, before the liberation.
And we were with my mother and some other ladies, digging a hole where Germans keep potatoes.
You know, they keep potatoes in-- they make small hills of, of, of soil.
And they bury inside the potatoes.
The Germans have these because they eat a lot of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], potatoes.
And my mother and the other ladies with their fingers, digging out potatoes.
And suddenly, we hear tanks.
And we thought it were German tanks.
And we started running back to where this train was.
And suddenly, they saw that they were Americans.
And you can imagine the kisses and the hugs that these American soldiers and officers received.
This was how we were liberated.
He was in the camp, trying to find food all the time.
Yes, he was with us.
He was with us.
We're together.
We were free.
They left us there.
We didn't know what to do.
We were free, we were not free.
The, the war had ended.
And it didn't-- we didn't know until the American Army passed this forest and found us there.
They just found us there.
Where there were air attacks, the Germans would take us out of the train.
They will put us under the trees.
And since I wore a red coat, I remember a German soldier telling my mother to take it off, because they could see the red coat.
The airplanes were flying so low.
And my mother wouldn't let him, because she had the ring hidden in my shoulder pad.
She told this after the war, of course she told me.
No, I didn't.
Because I was a child, I would have, I would have said it.
I, I didn't know the value of a diamond that could save my life afterwards.
The date, it was the 13th of April, 1945, there, in the middle of the forest.
Yes, Sunday.
